content:
Suppose C is a finitely complete category with other properties to be determined, and T is a lex, idempotent comonad on C. When is C the slice of the subcategory of T-modal objects over the image of R(1), for R the coreflector, such that the comonad is the base change/∀ adjunction?

This is something like a stripped-down version of asking when a topos E with a lex comonad is essential over the category of coalgebras for the comonad.
I found the question hard to parse, partly because I don't know what a T-modal object is, but it seems like a question I should be a le to answer if I can understand it

Sorry, that was indeed a bit cryptic. Given the idempotent comonad TTT on CCC, consider the full subcategory CTC_TCT​ of objects XXX such that the counit T(X)→XT(X) \to XT(X)→X is an isomorphism. These are the "modal objects". We have a coreflector R ⁣:C→CTR\colon C\to C_TR:C→CT​, as CTC_TCT​ is a coreflective subcategory of CCC. Under what conditions is CCC equivalence to a slice of CTC_TCT​? Specifically CT/R(1)C_T/R(1)CT​/R(1), which if I have it correct, reproduces the case C=E/AC=E/AC=E/A for EEE a finitely complete category and AAA any object. In this case, R ⁣:E/A→ER\colon E/A\to ER:E/A→E is the forgetful functor, its left adjoint is given by A∗(B)=A×BA^*(B) = A\times BA∗(B)=A×B, and TTT is the comonad on E/AE/AE/A given by A∗∘∀A ⁣:E/A→E/AA^*\circ \forall_A\colon E/A\to E/AA∗∘∀A​:E/A→E/A. Further, 1=idA1 = id_A1=idA​ and so then R(1)=AR(1)=AR(1)=A.

If EEE is a topos then we have the essential geometric morphism E/A→EE/A\to EE/A→E, but I'm only interested in the case when say EEE is a LCCC or what-have-you (just enough to get ∀A\forall_A∀A​ etc)
(Any reason for saying "T-modal objects" rather than "T-coalgebras"?)

Something seems messed up in the description. Under the normal meaning, the forgetful functor E/A→AE/A \to AE/A→A has a right adjoint given by A∗(B)=(π:A×B→A)A^\ast(B) = (\pi: A \times B \to A)A∗(B)=(π:A×B→A). The comonad would be the functor A×−:E→EA \times -: E \to EA×−:E→E, and the category of coalgebras indeed gives you the slice E/AE/AE/A. I'm not sure how the dependent product gets brought in.

@Todd Trimble Arg, yes. I don't mean forgetful functor, I mean ∀\forall∀!

@Nathanael Arkor because I was coming from a modal logic perspective, where I'm not assuming the semantics of the lex idempotent comonad are a priori coming from a slice category as given on the nLab page. I'm wondering if that can be recovered.

Middle of the night here, will try to rewrite in the morning.
Thanks for everyone being patient
R(1) should of course be 1, so that T(1)=A

This is probably bad timing then, it being in the wee hours for you, but the example of a comonad TTT you produced, A∗∘∀A ⁣:E/A→E/AA^*\circ \forall_A\colon E/A\to E/AA∗∘∀A​:E/A→E/A, doesn't look like idempotent to me. Relatedly, the functor A∗A^*A∗ isn't fully faithful for most objects.

OK, I'm really having a bad run of luck here :-)

I think I was mislead by notation at the nLab page [[necessity and possibility]], where it uses the notation H/W\mathbf{H}_{/W}H/W​ and calls it "the category of types in context WWW". Some paragraphs down the confession is made that □\Box□ is not idempotent "if generalized from first-order hyperdoctrines to more general dependent type theories.". At the start of the discussion it's said that H/(−)\mathbf{H}_{/(-)}H/(−)​ "might" be a hyperdoctrine.

I really should have thought it through, and I guess this is what I want: given a finitely complete category CCC with a lex comonad TTT, when can I recognise that C≃E/AC\simeq E/AC≃E/A and the comonad is the one from base change and dependent product (or ∀\forall∀, either would be interesting). Obviously we need the comonad to have a left adjoint, but can we figure out how to see this from seemingly more basic assumptions?

I've been thinking about this a little. It's easier for me to think about the induced monad on the slice category (I'll call it MMM) so I'll do that for now.
MMM sends f:X→Af:X\to Af:X→A to π2:X×A→A\pi_2:X \times A \to Aπ2​:X×A→A; the unit is the graph of fff. That means that an algebra is a morphism α:X×A→X\alpha: X \times A \to Xα:X×A→X such that

  • f(α(x,a))=af(\alpha(x,a))=af(α(x,a))=a (condition to be a morphism over AAA)
  • α(x,f(x))=x\alpha(x,f(x))=xα(x,f(x))=x (condition to be a retraction of the unit)
  • α(α(x,a),b)=α(x,b)\alpha(\alpha(x,a),b) = \alpha(x,b)α(α(x,a),b)=α(x,b) (compatibility with multiplication)

The first condition forces fff to be epic, and being a split epi is sufficient for an algebra structure to exist. Not too surprising.

The reason for considering this is that the category of MMM-algebras is the only reconstructible replacement for EEE once we have only the slice and the induced monad. How much of EEE can we recover?

There is a canonical comparison functor from EEE to algebras sending YYY to the free algebra (π2:Y×A→A,π13)(\pi_2:Y \times A\to A, \pi_{13})(π2​:Y×A→A,π13​). This functor need not be faithful (consider a case where AAA is an object of a topos which is not well-supported), but we can still consider whether C≃CM/(idA,π2)C \simeq C^M/(\mathrm{id}_A, \pi_2)C≃CM/(idA​,π2​), where the latter is (isomorphic to) the free algebra on the terminal object of EEE.

Note that the object being sliced over already has underlying object the terminal object of CCC, so there is at most one algebra homomorphism for any algebra, and there is a faithful functor from right to left... But we saw above that we only have an algebra structure on those fff which were well-supported, so there will typically be objects of CCC which aren't recoverable from the slice of the algebras.

That's not the end of the story, since there may be some subtle differences when we try to slice the coalgebras of the adjoint comonad TTT, but my intuition is that we can't generally recover enough of EEE from one of its slices to definitively characterize slice categories in term of the induced monad/comonad alone.

I'm too distraught if we can't - and indeed, the point is that if we have some semantic model for modal logic in the necessity/possibility sense, unless we assume all the bells and whistles (S5 + maybe more), it's very likely the case that it's too strong to ask for the semantics to be the adjoint pair of monad/comonad on a slice category, which for the case of Set/A, we are really thinking of sheaves on a discrete space. Even for S4 modal logic, we have to generalise to something like semantics in sheaves on a Stone space with a continuous relation on it (if I have understood/translated the literature correctly).

What to me seems interesting is the problem of reconstructing what the semantics is from just knowing the modalities, which would then show the kind of dependent-types semantics as @David Corfield so eloquently developed in his book is in some sense inevitable, once you decide on some sort of categorical semantics.

Even just being able to reconstruct A up to isomorphism from E/A + (co)monad (maybe both? Feels very Hegelian) seems like an interesting challenge: what do we need to be able to "find the set of possible worlds"? Phrasing it like that I guess shows that from merely □\Box□ and ♢\diamondsuit♢ it pretty much should be impossible. But what do we need on top of the modal operators to get there?
